title: Bifteki
subtitle: Greek grilled beef patties with feta and herbs
excerpt: Large, loosely packed beef patties seasoned with red onion, dried oregano, and parsley, with a hidden pocket of feta in the centre. Grilled over high heat until charred outside and still juicy within. Served with lemon, Greek salad, and pitta.
sourceNotes: Bifteki (μπιφτέκι) derives from the Turkish köfte tradition but has become wholly Greek in character. The feta-stuffed version, often called bifteki yemisto, is a modern Greek taverna standard. The dish appears across Greek grilling culture from the 1950s onwards as part of the taverna menu.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze uncooked patties on a tray, then transfer to bags. Defrost in the fridge overnight and grill from cold.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shape and refrigerate the patties up to 24 hours ahead; the resting time firms them and improves flavour.

content.text: Bifteki is not a Greek version of a burger. It is larger, looser, and flatter than a burger patty, it is grilled rather than fried in most cases, and the seasoning is distinctly Greek: oregano, onion, and parsley rather than Worcestershire and mustard. The feta goes inside the patty, where it melts partially during grilling and seasons the meat from within. Do not pack the mince tightly or the patty will be dense; bifteki should have some texture.

Use beef mince with at least 15% fat. Lean mince produces a dry, crumbly result. If you can get a 50/50 mix of beef and lamb mince, use it — the lamb adds a deeper, slightly gamier note that is closer to the traditional taverna version.

Combine the {{beef-mince}}, {{red-onion}}, {{garlic}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{oregano-dried}}, {{breadcrumbs-fresh}}, {{eggs}} egg, salt, and pepper in a bowl. Mix with your hands until just combined — do not overwork. Divide into 4 equal portions. Press each into an oval about 2 cm thick, place a quarter of the {{feta}} in the centre, and fold and press the meat around the cheese to seal. Shape into a flat oval again. Refrigerate for 30 minutes to firm up.

Heat a grill, griddle, or barbecue to high. Brush the patties with a little olive oil. Grill for 5 to 6 minutes per side until well charred outside and just cooked through — a faint pink interior is fine as long as the patty has been at temperature throughout. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve with {{lemon}} wedges and warm {{pitta-bread}}.

items.symptom: Patties have fallen apart on the grill
items.cause: Too much moisture in the mix, or not chilled before grilling
items.fix: Squeeze the grated onion in a cloth before adding; refrigerate shaped patties for at least 30 minutes; handle gently when turning

items.cause: Meat not sealed properly around the cheese
items.fix: Press firmly around the edges after enclosing the feta; use dry, crumbled feta rather than wet brined cubes
Mix until the ingredients are just combined — visible chunks of parsley and onion in the mix are fine
Heat the griddle until it smokes before adding the patties; do not move them for the first 4 minutes so a proper crust develops

content.text: Bifteki is the taverna's contribution to the Greek grill repertoire: simpler than a kleftiko, faster than a roast lamb, more substantial than souvlaki. Greek tavernas keep it on the menu year-round, but it is a warm-season dish at heart — grilled outside over wood or charcoal, eaten with salad and retsina in the early evening. The feta-stuffed version became widespread in the 1970s and 1980s as Greek taverna cooking reached its modern form. The name derives from the Ottoman biftek, itself borrowed from French bifteck, the whole word tracing a migration from England to France to Istanbul to Athens.
